title: Nanoscale Fluorescence Thermometry: Probes, Recent Advances and Emerging Directions
authors: Md Shakhawath Hossain, Nhat Minh Nguyen, Thi Ngoc Anh Mai, Trung Vuong Doan, Chaohao Chen, Qian Peter Su, Jiayan Liao, Yongliang Chen, Quynh Le-Van, Vu Khac Dat, Toan Dinh, Xiaoxue Xu, Toan Trong Tran
abstract: The transition of materials and devices to nanometer, atomic, and quantum scales makes thermal characterization increasingly challenging, driving the need for advanced nanoscale thermometry. Fluorescence nanothermometry has emerged as a powerful approach, enabling remote, spatially resolved temperature measurements with sub-micrometer-to-nanometer precision across applications in nanoelectronics, microfluidics, and biological systems. In these systems, temperature is inferred from variations in fluorescence observables, including spectral position, intensity, linewidth, and excited-state dynamics. This review provides a comprehensive and critical overview of fluorescence nanothermometry, covering fundamental mechanisms, material platforms, recent advances, and emerging applications. It further presents a critical evaluation of key challenges and discusses emerging strategies and future research directions toward achieving robust, real-time thermometry. It is anticipated that this review will stimulate further advances in material platforms and system design, accelerating the development of accurate, scalable, and application-ready nanoscale thermometers.
summary: This review examines the field of fluorescence nanothermometry, a technique used to measure temperature at the nanoscale without physical contact. It covers various material platforms like diamond color centers and quantum dots, detailing how changes in light properties can reveal thermal information. The paper highlights recent advances in using machine learning and multi-parametric sensing to improve precision. It also addresses critical challenges like thermal quenching and the need for robust calibration in complex environments.
main_problem: The challenge of performing accurate thermal characterization at the nanometer, atomic, and quantum scales where conventional contact-based thermometry fails due to thermal loading and limited spatial resolution.
main_result: The review provides a comprehensive taxonomy of fluorescence nanothermometry, highlighting the transition from diffusive to quasi-ballistic heat transport and the advanced use of diamond color centers for high-precision sensing.
method: A critical review of optical sensing modalities, including fluorescence, Raman, and IR thermography, alongside a comparison of spin-based (ODMR) and all-optical detection techniques.
model_or_system: The primary platforms discussed are fluorescence-based nanoprobes, specifically diamond color centers (NV, SiV, SnV, etc.), quantum dots, and upconversion nanoparticles, applied to nanoelectronics, microfluidics, and biological systems.
key_observables: Fluorescence intensity, spectral position (ZPL), linewidth, fluorescence lifetime, and zero-field splitting (ZFS) parameters.
important_parameters: Spatial resolution (sub-nanometer to micrometer), temporal resolution, temperature range (15 K to 1000 K), and thermal sensitivity metrics (absolute and relative).
main_assumptions: The review notes limitations such as the need for calibration in specific environments, potential laser-induced self-heating, and the breakdown of Fourier's law in the quasi-ballistic regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates various micro/nanoscale thermometry strategies; Figure 2 shows bibliometric trends in publications and citations; Figure 3 details the structure and energy levels of NV centers and their application in intracellular sensing.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the necessity of non-contact thermometry, classifies various techniques by contact and signal nature, reviews specific material platforms (focusing on diamond), discusses advanced sensing modes (spin-based vs. all-optical), and concludes with future directions and challenges.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
